findings.dominantFinding: The dominant imaging finding is a large, irregularly shaped intraparenchymal hemorrhage located in the parasagittal subcortical white matter. On unenhanced CT, there may be associated hyperdensity within the adjacent superior sagittal sinus.
findings.supportingFeatures: Supporting features include significant surrounding vasogenic edema and involvement of the overlying cortex, which frequently crosses expected arterial boundaries. If contrast is administered, a characteristic 'empty delta' sign representing a filling defect surrounded by enhancing dura may be visible within the superior sagittal sinus.
findings.criticalNegatives: Importantly, there is no evidence of an underlying discrete solid enhancing mass to suggest a hemorrhagic neoplasm. Furthermore, there is no classic 'popcorn' appearance with a complete hemosiderin rim on MRI to suggest a cavernoma, and no tangle of serpentine flow voids indicative of an arteriovenous malformation.
findings.influentialFeature: The feature that most influences my thinking is the bilateral parasagittal location of the hemorrhage crossing arterial territories, combined with a hyperdense superior sagittal sinus.

differentials.primaryDiagnosis: The appearances are most in keeping with Hemorrhagic infarct (venous infarct secondary to sagittal sinus thrombosis), given the parasagittal distribution and the characteristic hyperdensity in the superior sagittal sinus. Venous infarctions classically occur when venous outflow obstruction leads to profound local venous hypertension, resulting in ischemia that frequently undergoes hemorrhagic transformation.
differentials.principleDifferential: The principle differential would be Vascular malformation, however cavernous malformations typically present as focal, well-defined lesions with mixed-signal blood products and little surrounding edema unless acutely ruptured. Furthermore, arteriovenous malformations would demonstrate prominent feeding arteries and draining veins without the characteristic dural sinus thrombosis seen here.
differentials.excludeDiagnosis: A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Hemorrhagic neoplasm, given that brain tumors such as metastases or glioblastoma can present with acute hemorrhage, and missing this would drastically alter oncological management. However, hemorrhagic neoplasms typically exhibit a distinct, irregular enhancing solid component and disproportionately massive vasogenic edema, rather than the diffuse cortical-subcortical infarction pattern seen with venous occlusion.

management.priority: From a radiological perspective, the priority is to definitively confirm the presence and extent of the dural venous sinus thrombosis and closely monitor for life-threatening mass effect or herniation.
management.priorImaging: I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to establish whether the patient had pre-existing dural sinus narrowing, underlying hypercoagulable states, or a recent normal baseline.
management.furtherImaging: I would recommend a CT venogram (CTV) or MR venogram (MRV), as this modality is superior here for exquisitely demonstrating the filling defect within the superior sagittal sinus and assessing the collateral venous drainage.
management.mriSequences: I would specify T2* gradient echo or susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) sequences to highly sensitively detect intracellular blood products and intravascular thrombus, alongside contrast-enhanced MR venography to map the exact extent of the occluded sinuses.
management.mdtDiscussion: This should go to the Neurology and Neurosurgery MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of the sagittal sinus thrombosis, the volume of the hemorrhagic infarct, and any signs of impending midline shift or herniation to guide urgent anticoagulation and possible decompressive surgery.

vivaAnswer.fullScript:
The dominant imaging finding is a large, irregularly shaped intraparenchymal hemorrhage located in the parasagittal subcortical white matter. On unenhanced CT, there may be associated hyperdensity within the adjacent superior sagittal sinus. Supporting features include significant surrounding vasogenic edema and involvement of the overlying cortex, which frequently crosses expected arterial boundaries. If contrast is administered, a characteristic 'empty delta' sign representing a filling defect surrounded by enhancing dura may be visible within the superior sagittal sinus. Importantly, there is no evidence of an underlying discrete solid enhancing mass to suggest a hemorrhagic neoplasm. Furthermore, there is no classic 'popcorn' appearance with a complete hemosiderin rim on MRI to suggest a cavernoma, and no tangle of serpentine flow voids indicative of an arteriovenous malformation. The feature that most influences my thinking is the bilateral parasagittal location of the hemorrhage crossing arterial territories, combined with a hyperdense superior sagittal sinus.

The appearances are most in keeping with Hemorrhagic infarct (venous infarct secondary to sagittal sinus thrombosis), given the parasagittal distribution and the characteristic hyperdensity in the superior sagittal sinus. Venous infarctions classically occur when venous outflow obstruction leads to profound local venous hypertension, resulting in ischemia that frequently undergoes hemorrhagic transformation. The principle differential would be Vascular malformation, however cavernous malformations typically present as focal, well-defined lesions with mixed-signal blood products and little surrounding edema unless acutely ruptured. Furthermore, arteriovenous malformations would demonstrate prominent feeding arteries and draining veins without the characteristic dural sinus thrombosis seen here. A diagnosis I would want to exclude is Hemorrhagic neoplasm, given that brain tumors such as metastases or glioblastoma can present with acute hemorrhage, and missing this would drastically alter oncological management. However, hemorrhagic neoplasms typically exhibit a distinct, irregular enhancing solid component and disproportionately massive vasogenic edema, rather than the diffuse cortical-subcortical infarction pattern seen with venous occlusion.

From a radiological perspective, the priority is to definitively confirm the presence and extent of the dural venous sinus thrombosis and closely monitor for life-threatening mass effect or herniation. I would review any available prior imaging to assess disease behaviour — whether this is stable, progressive, or improving. Comparison for interval change is useful to establish whether the patient had pre-existing dural sinus narrowing, underlying hypercoagulable states, or a recent normal baseline. I would recommend a CT venogram (CTV) or MR venogram (MRV), as this modality is superior here for exquisitely demonstrating the filling defect within the superior sagittal sinus and assessing the collateral venous drainage. I would specify T2* gradient echo or susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) sequences to highly sensitively detect intracellular blood products and intravascular thrombus, alongside contrast-enhanced MR venography to map the exact extent of the occluded sinuses. This should go to the Neurology and Neurosurgery MDT. My report would clearly document the extent of the sagittal sinus thrombosis, the volume of the hemorrhagic infarct, and any signs of impending midline shift or herniation to guide urgent anticoagulation and possible decompressive surgery.
